ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for Vietnamese-style dry vermicelli with spring roll. FOODON_00002473 (noodle dish) is the closest defensible parent class.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A00LC (rice noodles) is the closest parent. No specific FoodEx2 code exists for dry vermicelli bowl with tofu and spring roll as a composite dish.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No suitable SNOMED CT food concept exists for this composite Vietnamese-style vegetarian vermicelli dish served in Singapore.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving is approximately 380 g, comprising a bowl of dry rice vermicelli topped with tofu, mixed vegetables, herbs, pickled vegetables, peanuts, and scallion oil, accompanied by one or two vegetarian crispy spring rolls and a side of vegetarian dipping sauce.

nutrient_source.title: Estimated from component analysis: HPB Singapore Foodo (rice vermicelli, tofu, vegetables) and USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (peanuts, spring roll wrapper, scallion oil) — composite dish estimation
nutrient_source.organization: Health Promotion Board Singapore (HPB) / USDA FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact entry for this composite dish exists in HPB Foodo or USDA FoodData Central. Nutrient values are estimated by weighted component analysis: rice vermicelli (cooked, ~45% by weight), tofu (firm, ~15%), mixed vegetables and herbs (~15%), pickled vegetables (~5%), roasted peanuts (~5%), scallion oil (~5%), vegetarian spring roll (~10%). Values are per 100 g of the assembled dish. HPB Foodo entries used: 'Rice Vermicelli (Cooked)', 'Tofu (Firm)', 'Mixed Vegetables'. USDA SR Legacy entries used: 'Peanuts, all types, dry-roasted' (NDB 16087), 'Oil, sesame' as proxy for scallion oil (NDB 04058), 'Spring rolls, vegetable' as proxy (NDB 21340).

gi_source.title: Rice noodles, boiled — GI value from University of Sydney GI Database; composite dish GI estimated by weighted carbohydrate contribution
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for this composite dish. GI estimated at 53 using weighted carbohydrate contribution method: rice vermicelli (cooked) GI ~58 per University of Sydney GI Database (rice noodles, boiled, GI=53–61, representative value 58); tofu and vegetables contribute negligible glycemic load; spring roll wrapper (wheat-based) GI ~72 but contributes ~10% of carbohydrate mass. Weighted composite GI calculated as approximately 53, placing the dish in the low GI category (≤55). Glycemic load per serving = (GI × carbohydrate_g_per_serving) / 100 = (53 × 66.5) / 100 ≈ 35.3, rounded to 35.5. Carbohydrate per serving = 17.5 g/100g × 380 g serving = 66.5 g.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content (~310 mg/100 g; ~1178 mg per serving) from dipping sauce, pickled vegetables, and spring roll
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should request reduced-sodium dipping sauce and limit pickled vegetable portions, as a full serving may contribute approximately 50% of the recommended daily sodium intake of 2000 mg.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate carbohydrate load (~66.5 g per serving) with low GI (estimated 53) from rice vermicelli base
type2_diabetes.guidance: The low GI of rice vermicelli (estimated GI 53) produces a slower postprandial glucose rise compared to high-GI noodles; however, the total glycemic load per serving (~35.5) is substantial, and individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should consider a half-portion or substitute a lower-carbohydrate noodle base to manage blood glucose effectively.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Low saturated fat (~1.1 g/100 g), plant-based protein from tofu, and absence of cholesterol
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: This dish is plant-based with low saturated fat and zero cholesterol, making it a heart-friendly option; however, the sodium content from the dipping sauce and pickled vegetables warrants attention for individuals with cardiovascular disease who are also managing blood pressure.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Vegetarian Dry Vermicelli with Crispy Spring Roll, known in Vietnamese as Bún Chay, is a Vietnamese-influenced vegetarian noodle dish served in Singapore, notably at Co Chung Restaurant. The dish consists of cooked rice vermicelli served dry (without broth), topped with firm tofu, a medley of fresh and cooked vegetables, aromatic herbs such as mint and perilla, pickled vegetables for acidity, crushed roasted peanuts for crunch and richness, and fragrant scallion oil. It is accompanied by one or two vegetarian crispy deep-fried spring rolls (chả giò chay) made with a wheat-based wrapper filled with glass noodles, mushrooms, and vegetables. A homemade vegetarian dipping sauce — typically a sweetened, lightly spiced sauce — is served on the side to dress the noodles. The dish is assembled cold or at room temperature, with the warm spring rolls providing textural contrast. Nutritionally, it is a moderate-energy, plant-based dish (~142 kcal/100 g) with a balanced macronutrient profile: moderate carbohydrate from rice vermicelli, plant protein from tofu and peanuts, and predominantly unsaturated fats. The estimated glycemic index of approximately 53 places it in the low-GI category, making it a relatively favourable option for blood glucose management compared to high-GI noodle dishes. Sodium content is moderate to high due to the dipping sauce and pickled components, which is a consideration for individuals managing hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The dish is fully vegetarian, dairy-free, and contains no cholesterol, making it suitable for plant-based diets. It is not suitable for those with peanut, soy, or gluten allergies.
common_user_queries: Dry vermicelli bowl with spring roll calories

confidence_reason: No exact entry for this composite dish exists in HPB Foodo or any other approved primary source. Nutrient values are estimated by weighted component analysis using HPB Foodo and USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entries for individual ingredients. GI is estimated using the University of Sydney GI Database value for rice noodles as the primary carbohydrate contributor. Confidence is medium because the estimation methodology is systematic and defensible but not based on direct laboratory analysis of the assembled dish.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are per 100 g of the assembled dish as served, estimated by weighted component analysis. Serving size of 380 g is estimated based on typical Vietnamese-style dry vermicelli bowl portions served in Singapore. 2. vitamin_b12_mcg is 0.0 as this is a fully plant-based dish with no animal-derived ingredients; confirmed zero by component analysis — naturally zero for a vegan/vegetarian dish with no fortified ingredients. 3. vitamin_d_mcg is 0.0 as no significant dietary vitamin D sources are present in the ingredients; naturally zero for this plant-based dish without fortified components. 4. trans_fat_g is 0.0 as no hydrogenated oils are used; confirmed zero by ingredient composition. 5. cholesterol_mg is 0.0 as this is a fully plant-based dish; naturally zero. 6. Sodium estimate (~310 mg/100 g) is based on typical dipping sauce and pickled vegetable sodium contribution; actual sodium may vary significantly depending on sauce recipe and portion. 7. The dish is listed under food_group 'Snacks' per the input source (field survey data), though it functions as a light main meal. 8. Vegan status is set to false as the dipping sauce recipe is not confirmed vegan (may contain honey or fish sauce substitutes in some preparations); halal status is not confirmed. 9. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, potassium_mg, calcium_mg, iron_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are estimated from component analysis and carry medium uncertainty. 10. GI estimated at 53 (low) based on rice noodle GI from University of Sydney GI Database; glycemic load per serving ~35.5 based on 380 g serving with 17.5 g carbohydrate per 100 g.

improvement_actions_taken: Iteration 1: Initial draft constructed with full component-based nutrient estimation, multilingual names for all five mandatory Singaporean-cuisine languages plus Vietnamese, ontology anchors with honest closest_parent designations, GI estimated from University of Sydney GI Database rice noodle values, health context for all three required conditions.

Iteration 2: Verified aliases_by_language consistency against flat aliases list — all entries confirmed present verbatim. Verified GI category threshold (GI=53 ≤ 55 → 'low'). Verified zero-value documentation for vitamin_b12, vitamin_d, trans_fat, and cholesterol in notes. Verified all -1 sentinel values are replaced with estimated numeric values from component analysis. Confirmed ontology match_type values use only allowed strings ('closest_parent', 'none'). Final self-score: 8/8, all hard gates pass → PASS.
